coredata.dc:title: Multifunctional carbon dots with full-band UV-to-HEBL shielding and antibacterial properties in polyvinyl alcohol film for enhancing food preservation

coredata.dc:description: In this study, a new type of carbon dots based on diethyl ferulate (DEF-CDs) was developed, and doped into polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) to prepare an active food packaging film that effectively inhibits photooxidation and microbial contamination of food. Benefit from the rational design of the structure and optimization of the precursor for the DEF-CDs synthesis, 0.4 % of DEF-CDs in PVA film can prevent 100 % ultraviolet light (UV) and 99.90 % high-energy blue light (HEBL). Furthermore, its UV and HEBL blocking capacity shows no significant decline under 40 days of accelerated aging tests. Moreover, the DEF-CDs/PVA film exhibits significant antibacterial capacity, and with the concentration of DEF-CDs increased from 0 % to 0.8 %, the inhibition zone diameters increased from 8 mm to 22.8 mm for Staphylococcus aureus and from 8 mm to 11.8 mm for Escherichia coli O157:H7. Finally, using the DEF-CDs/PVA film as active food packaging, the shelf life of strawberry, jujube and milk was extended significantly. This work exhibits the potential of DEF-CDs as multifunctional nanoreinforcements for constructing protective food packaging.

head.ce:title: Multifunctional carbon dots with full-band UV-to-HEBL shielding and antibacterial properties in polyvinyl alcohol film for enhancing food preservation

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, a new type of carbon dots based on diethyl ferulate (DEF-CDs) was developed, and doped into polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) to prepare an active food packaging film that effectively inhibits photooxidation and microbial contamination of food. Benefit from the rational design of the structure and optimization of the precursor for the DEF-CDs synthesis, 0.4 % of DEF-CDs in PVA film can prevent 100 % ultraviolet light (UV) and 99.90 % high-energy blue light (HEBL). Furthermore, its UV and HEBL blocking capacity shows no significant decline under 40 days of accelerated aging tests. Moreover, the DEF-CDs/PVA film exhibits significant antibacterial capacity, and with the concentration of DEF-CDs increased from 0 % to 0.8 %, the inhibition zone diameters increased from 8 mm to 22.8 mm for Staphylococcus aureus and from 8 mm to 11.8 mm for Escherichia coli O157:H7. Finally, using the DEF-CDs/PVA film as active food packaging, the shelf life of strawberry, jujube and milk was extended significantly. This work exhibits the potential of DEF-CDs as multifunctional nanoreinforcements for constructing protective food packaging.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Food spoilage is a serious global problem. According to the statistics from the Food and Agriculture Organization, an estimated 931 million tons of food is wasted in households, retailers, restaurants and other food services (Patra, Henley, Benefo, Pradhan, & Shirmohammadi, 2022). Generally, microorganisms, light and oxidation reactions are the main reasons for food spoilage (Alizadeh Sani, Khezerlou, Tavassoli, Abedini, & McClements, 2024; Sun, et al., 2022). Food packaging is one of the most important and simple measures to reduce food waste and maintain food quality (Cazón & Vázquez, 2021). However, the traditional plastic materials used in food packaging, such as polyethylene terephthalate (PET), polypropylene (PP), and polyethylene (PE) are mainly used as passive barriers to prevent contaminant intrusion (Drago, Campardelli, Pettinato, & Perego, 2020; Sid, Mor, Kishore, & Sharanagat, 2021). In addition, the production of micro- and nano-plastics from traditional non-biodegradable plastics is an ecological challenge that has aroused great concern due to the scope of its environmental pollution (Gondal, Bhat, Gómez, Areche, & Huaman, 2023; Mustapha, et al., 2024; S. Zhang, et al., 2021; Y. Zhao et al., 2023). Therefore, development of active and biodegradable packaging to enhance food shelf life and thus improve the quality and safety of food, as well as reduce plastic pollution, is urgently required.

In recent years, a large number of biodegradable materials including polylactic acid, poly (butyleneadipate-co-terephthalate), cellulose, chitosan, and polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) have been introduced in the food packaging industry to replace non-biodegradable plastics (Shaikh, Yaqoob, & Aggarwal, 2021; M. Zhang, et al., 2022). Among these, PVA has obtained wide usage in clinical and daily chemical applications, due to such distinctive properties as hydrophilicity, biocompatibility and low cost (Karimzadeh, Mahmoudpour, Rahimpour, & Jouyban, 2022; Oun, Shin, Rhim, & Kim, 2022; Suganthi, Vignesh, Kalyana Sundar, & Raj, 2020; D. Yang, et al., 2023). Therefore, PVA is considered to have great potential application in food packaging. However, its poor thermal stability, high light transmittance and water-solubility do not meet the essential criteria for food packaging, therefore limiting its further application (Abral, et al., 2020; Suleiman, Zeng, Chakma, Wakai, & Feng, 2024; Tian, et al., 2023). However, incorporation of functional nanomaterials in the preparation of composite PVA films is one of the most effective approaches to enhance PVA performance (Toyoda & Yamamoto, 2018; Yan, et al., 2024).

Carbon dots (CDs), as a new class of carbon-based nanomaterial with diameters less than 10 nm (Zhao, Zhang, Wang, & Devahastin, 2020), are widely applied due to their excellent optical performance (Xia, Zhu, Feng, Yang, & Yang, 2019), stable aqueous dispersion (B. Li, et al., 2021), low toxicity and high biocompatibility (Khan, Ezati, & Rhim, 2023). Recently, there has been an increasing number of reports on remarkable antibacterial activity of carbon dots, thus highlighting their potential as a promising antibacterial agent in food packaging (P. Sun, et al., 2024; Zhao, Zhang, Mujumdar, & Wang, 2023). Even more importantly, many studies have demonstrated the UV absorption capability of carbon dots (Hess, et al., 2017; Sul, Khan, & Rhim, 2024). For example, using citric acid monohydrate, ethylene diamine and hydrochloric acid to synthesize N-doped carbon dots (NCDs), 0.05 wt% of NCDs in cellulose film can achieve 94 % UV-A and 80 % UV-B blockage (Barman, Sele Handegård, Hashimoto, & Nagao, 2021). However, achieving full-band UV shielding and maintaining good transparency at visible wavelengths remains a challenge in the present study. The majority of reported CDs only exhibit absorption in either the UV-A or UV-B region, and relatively low UV blocking capacity (Chen, et al., 2022). Generally, the optical performance of CDs relies on the precursor (Mintz, Zhou, & Leblanc, 2019). In theory, the CDs synthesized using compounds rich in benzene rings and conjugated double bonds often exhibit good UV absorption capacity (Chen, et al., 2022). Ferulate ester, a natural plant chemical with aromatic rings and adjacent carbon-carbon double bonds (Cockell, 1998; Karamać, Koleva, Kancheva, & Amarowicz, 2017; Ou & Kwok, 2004), has strong ultraviolet absorption properties (Joram Mendoza, et al., 2020). Therefore, employing ferulate ester as the precursor for synthesizing CDs is a promising strategy to achieve full-band UV shielding.

In this study, a new environmental friendly multifunctional food packaging material was developed. First, a new type of carbon dots based on ferulate ester derivatives was developed, which exhibited full-band UV and high-energy blue light (UV-to-HEBL) shielding and antibacterial properties. Then incorporation of the CDs into PVA films demonstrate their enhanced structural and potential functional properties in food packaging. To demonstrate the preservation effects of the prepared composite film in practical applications, storage experiments on strawberry, jujube and milk were carried out. A new direction for design and development of multifunctional food packaging materials has been illuminated by our work.

Sodium citrate (CA-Na), ethyl ferulate (EF), ethylenediamine (EDA), riboflavin, indole-3-carboxaldehyde, uric acid and lumichrome were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (Saint Louis, MO, USA). Diethyl ferulate (DEF) was purchased from Hesichem Pharmaceutical (Beijing, China). Polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) powder (1799, alcoholysis degree of 98–99 %) was purchased from Titan Technology (Shanghai, China). Escherichia coli O157:H7 (E. coli O157:H7, CICC 10907) and Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus, ATCC 25923) were from China Center of Industrial Culture Collection (Beijing, China). Fresh strawberries, jujubes and pasteurized milk were purchased from a supermarket in Beijing, China. CdSe/ZnS quantum dots were purchased from Mesolight (Suzhou, China). All solutions were prepared with ultrapure water obtained using Elix-3 and Milli-QA (Molsheim, France). All other chemical reagents were analytical grade and used without further purification.

The EF-CDs and DEF-CDs were synthesized by a typical solvothermal method with minor modification (Esmaeili, et al., 2022). Briefly, 1.29 g of CA-Na, the desired amounts of EF and DEF, respectively, and desired volumes of EDA were added in 30 mL of deionized water and mixed for 3 min via ultrasound. Then, the mixed solution was transferred to a teflon autoclave and heated at 200 °C for 8 h. After the solution cooled to room temperature, the obtained mixtures were filtered with a film filter (0.22 μm) to remove the sediment, and dialyzed with a dialysis bag (molar weight cut-off: 1000 Da) for 24 h. Finally, the resultant EF-CDs and DEF-CDs were stored at 4 °C for further use.

The DEF-CDs/PVA films were prepared by a simple solvent casting method (Qiu, et al., 2023). First, 5 g of PVA powder were dissolved in 100 mL of deionized water and stirred at 95 °C for 2 h to obtain the PVA solution (5 %, v/v), and 500 μL of glycerol was used as a plasticizer to homogeneously mix with the PVA solution. Then, the PVA solution was mixed with different concentrations of DEF-CDs; the final concentrations of DEF-CDs were 0.0 wt%, 0.2 wt%, 0.4 wt% and 0.8 wt%, respectively. Finally, 5 mL of each DEF-CDs/PVA solution was poured into a 3 × 4 cm plastic plate and dried for 14 h at 50 °C. The prepared films were labeled PVA (no DEF-CDs), 0.2 % DEF-CDs/PVA, 0.4 % DEF-CDs/PVA and 0.8 % DEF-CDs/PVA, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical performance evaluation of DEF-CDs and DEF-CDs/PVA films
ce:section.ce:para:
The UV blocking, HEBL blocking and transmission capacity of the prepared films and CDs were evaluated within the range of 200 nm – 700 nm (UV–vis spectrum) (Wang, et al., 2017). The UV blocking efficiency (200 nm – 400 nm) of the films and CDs can be calculated using the following formula:
(1)
UV
blocking ( % ) = 100 −
∫ 200 400
T ( λ ) d λ
/
∫ 200 400
d λ （ % ）

The HEBL blocking efficiency (400 nm – 450 nm) of the films and CDs can be calculated using the following formula:
(2)
HEBL
blocking ( % ) = 100 −
∫ 400 450
T ( λ ) d λ
/
∫ 400 450
d λ （ % ）
where T(λ) is the average spectral transmittance of film, and dλ is bandwidth.

For optical performance of DEF-CDs/PVA films, 40 μg of riboflavin was dissolved in 1 mL of phosphate buffer saline (PBS) (0.01 M, pH 6.0) with ultrasonic assistance. The entire experiment was conducted under dark conditions. At regular intervals, the absorbance of the riboflavin solution at wavelength 445 nm was tested and the color change was recorded by digital camera. The UV shielding capacity of the DEF-CDs/PVA films is proportional to the absorbance of the riboflavin solution at 445 nm (Kim, Kim, Song, Kang, & Park, 2020).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Antibacterial capacity characterization of DEF-CDs/PVA films
ce:section.ce:para:
Two typical bacterial strains, E. coli O157:H7 (Gram-negative) and S. aureus (Gram-positive), were used to evaluate the antibacterial capacity of the films and CDs (C. Li, et al., 2023). For CDs, concretely, 50 μL of each respective test bacterial strain (108 CFU/mL), 1 mL of the proposed DEF-CDs, and 4 mL of Luria-Bertani (LB) sterile liquid medium (for E. coli O157:H7) and Tryptic soy broth (TSB) medium (for S. aureus), respectively, were fully mixed at 37 °C at 180 rpm for 12 h. The incubated bacterial solution was diluted to an appropriate concentration using sterile saline and coated on an agar plate, and incubated at 37 °C for 14 h. Colony forming units (CFU) were counted. Then, the antibacterial rate was calculated according to Eq. 3
(3)
antibacterial
rate ( % ) = ( 1 − CFU /
CFU 0
) × 100 %
where CFU0 is the number of colonies in the blank group, and CFU is the number of colonies in the experimental group.

The antibacterial properties of the DEF-CDs/PVA solutions were tested using inhibition zone experiments (K. Chen, et al., 2023; L. Zhang, et al., 2023). Briefly, a certain concentration of activated E.coli and S. aureus were uniformly applied on an agar plate, an Oxford cup (external diameter = 8 mm) was placed in the center of the agar plate, and DEF-CDs/PVA solutions of different concentrations were poured into the Oxford cup, which was cultured in a 37 °C incubator for 18 – 20 h. The diameter of the antibacterial zone was measured with a vernier caliper.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Jujube and strawberry preservation experiments using DEF-CDs/PVA films
ce:section.ce:para: Jujubes of the same size and maturity were selected for preservation experiment. The jujubes were individually put into paper cups and covered with DEF-CDs/PVA composite films at concentrations of 0.2 %、0.4 %、0.8 % DEF-CDs, respectively. The jujubes without cover were set as control. The experiment was performed under a UV light (365 nm) for seven days at room temperature, and the color changes exhibiting jujubes maturity were recorded. The hardness of jujubes was tested by a texture analyzer (FTC, America). The selected probe was needle-like, the test speed was 20 mm/min, and the trigger force was 1 N.

The same batch of strawberries with relatively consistent size and freshness were used after cleaning with ultrapure water, and let it air dry naturally. The strawberry preservation experiment was performed in similar with the jujubes. The appearance changes of the strawberries were recorded with a camera, and the weight was measured every other day. The weight loss rate was calculated by the weight loss divided by the initial weight of the sampling.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Milk preservation experiment using DEF-CDs/PVA film
ce:section.ce:para: Within a clean bench, 5 mL of milk was quickly poured into the transparent glass bottles, and then covered with pure PVA film, or 0.2 %, 0.4 % or 0.8 % DEF-CDs/PVA film, respectively. The packaged samples were then transferred to an artificial climate box with a temperature of 25 °C and illumination of 5000 Lux. The milk samples were tested 0, 12, 24, 48 and 72 h. Metabolite extraction was performed following the procedure described in our reported literature (D. Tan, et al., 2021). Briefly, 2 mL of milk was added into a 15 mL centrifuge tube, then centrifuged at 8000 rpm at 4 °C for 30 min. After lipid removal, 4 mL of acetonitrile was added, the solution was shaken for 5 min, and then centrifuged at 9500 rpm at 4 °C for 10 min to precipitate the macromolecular proteins. The supernatant was collected and filtered into a 2 mL brown injection bottle using 0.22 μm organic phase filter membrane for performance liquid chromatography analysis.

Ultraviolet-visible (UV–vis) absorption and transmission spectrums of films and CDs were recorded by a UV–vis spectrofluorometer (Q-6, METASH). High-resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM) graphics of CDs were recorded by a Tecnai G2 F30 S-TWIN electron microscope. Fourier-transform infrared (FTIR) spectral analyses of films and CDs were performed on a Nicolet IS10 FTIR spectrometer (Thermo Fisher). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) of CDs was recorded by X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (Thermo, ESCALAB, 250Xi). The X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern test of CDs was recorded by a Bruker D8 Advance XRD instrument. The surface morphologies and cross-section of the films were analyzed using an HITACHI Regulus 8100 Field Emission Scanning Electron Microscope, the instrument was operated at 3 kV. To test their cross-sections, the films were fractured under liquid nitrogen exposure. The thermal stabilities of the films were tested by a thermogravimetric analyzer (NETZSCH, STA 449 F3, Germany). The films were heated at a rate of 10 °C/min, with a heating range from 30 to 700 °C, in a pure nitrogen atmosphere with a gas flow of 25 mL/min. The tensile strength (TS) and elongation at break (EB) were analyzed using an electronic universal testing machine (SANS, CMT-6104) at the speed of 20 mm/min, and the films were cut into 10 mm × 50 mm rectangles for analysis. The water contact angle (WCA) of the DEF-CDs/PVA film was measured using a contact angle measuring instrument (Dataphysics, OCA50, Germany). Images of water droplets in contact with the film were recorded and the angles were calculated. Oxygen permeability (OP) of the films was measured using a permeability tester (GTR-701M, SYSTEST, China) according to a pressure difference method following a Chinese national standard (GB/T 1038–2022), the test was performed at 23 °C and 50 % RH.

All experiments were repeated three times. The data were shown as mean ± standard deviation (SD). The significance of the experimental data was analyzed (p < 0.05) with Duncan’s multiple range test and one-way analysis of variance (ANOVA) using the SPSS Statistics 26.0 (SPSS Inc., Chicago, IL, USA).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of ethyl ester group on structural and optical performance of ferulate ester CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The EF and DEF-based CDs were prepared using a one-step hydrothermal method. Interestingly, we found that the ethyl ester group on EF and DEF causes significant differences in the structure and optical properties of carbon dots. The EF-CDs and DEF-CDs were characterized by HRTEM. As shown in Fig. 1a-d, the DEF-CDs exhibit enhanced image contrast and uniform sphericity. The size histogram in Fig. S1 shows that the particle size of DEF-CDs ranges from 1.55 to 7.25 nm, with an average particle size of 3.48 ± 1.12 nm. Moreover, the DEF-CDs display a discernible lattice fringe of 0.21 nm corresponding to the (100) plane of graphite (Ai, et al., 2021), whereas it is challenging to observe any lattice structure in EF-CDs. These findings indicate that the DEF-CDs possess a greater electron density and stronger crystalline state than those of EF-CDs. This conclusion is also supported by the XRD results in Fig. 1e, the DEF-CDs and EF-CDs exhibit an obvious broad peak at 2θ = 19.4°, which is due to disordered carbon atoms with (100) planes (Bajpai, D’Souza, & Suhail, 2019). In addition, compared with EF-CDs, the diffraction peaks of DEF-CDs are relatively sharper, demonstrating the higher crystallinity of DEF-CDs. The prepared EF-CDs and DEF-CDs were further characterized by XPS spectra. The results depicted in Fig. 1f demonstrate that both EF-CDs and DEF-CDs exhibit three prominent peaks at 285.14 eV, 400.08 eV and 532.17 eV, corresponding to the C 1 s, N 1 s and O 1 s binding energies respectively (Zhou, Yang, & Zhang, 2013). Furthermore, as depicted in Fig. S2, the EF-CDs and DEF-CDs display indistinguishable high-resolution spectra of C 1 s and O 1 s, and are characterized by abundant CC/C-C, C-O, and CO/CN bonds (Sadhanala, et al., 2022; Sharma, Kirti, Kumari, Srivastava, & Paul, 2023; H. Zhang, et al., 2014). However, a significant difference in the high-resolution N 1 s spectra is found between EF-CDs and DEF-CDs; the EF-CDs exhibit substantial pyridine nitrogen, while a large amount of graphitic nitrogen is found in DEF-CDs (Fig. 1g-h) (F. Yang, Zhou, & Duan, 2021). Generally, a typical process for carbon dots formation contains three steps of polymerization, dehydration, and carbonization (Sagbas & Sahiner, 2019). The six-membered ring-like pyridine nitrogen is formed from the amino nitrogen in the oxidative polymerization reaction, which is then converted into graphite nitrogen when the degree of dehydration and carbonization is sufficient (Xian & Li, 2022). Therefore, the high-resolution spectra of N 1 s results further support that the DEF-CDs possess a stronger crystalline state than that of EF-CDs. The aforementioned results collectively demonstrate that the presence of ethyl ester in ferulate ester play a crucial role, and significantly influence the crystallization process of ferulate ester-based carbon dots.

The optical performances of EF and DEF-based CDs were compared. As shown in Fig. 1i, DEF contains an additional ethyl ester group. The UV–vis absorption spectra of EF and DEF were presented in Fig. S3, where EF exhibits an absorption intensity comparable to that of DEF. Notably, a distinct red shift is observed in DEF, which may be attributed to enhancement of electron cloud density of the benzene structure induced by the additional ester group. Furthermore, as shown in Fig. S4, the absorption peaks characteristic of the benzene ring of DEF in the range of 900 cm−1 to 650 cm−1 (bending vibration) disappeared after forming DEF-CDs (W. Zhang, et al., 2015). However, the discernible characteristic benzene ring absorption peaks in EF-CDs suggest that the initial EF structure is incompletely destroyed during the formation process. These structural differences between DEF-CDs and EF-CDs may have an impact on their optical performance. In Fig. 1j-k, the UV–vis absorbance spectra of the prepared EF-CDs and DEF-CDs depict that a prominent absorption in the UV region emerged in DEF-CDs, thus resulting in strong UV blocking capacity. Inversely, the EF-CDs exhibit poor UV blocking capacity due to weak absorption in UV region. This may be determined by the degree of carbon dots formation. The above results indicate that DEF is more suitable as a raw material for synthesizing high UV absorbing CDs.

In differentiation with the water-insoluble property of EF and DEF ferulate ester derivatives, after forming the CDs, both EF-CDs and DEF-CDs exhibit better water solubility (Fig. 1j-k inset), this may be due to the N and O precursors are enriched on the CDs surface to enhance charge accumulation. This is supported by FITR analysis in Fig. 1l. The prepared EF-CDs and DEF-CDs show a wide absorption band at 3421 cm−1 and 1586 cm−1, which is generated from the stretching vibration of N-H/O-H and CO bonds (Chen, et al., 2022; Lin, et al., 2019), respectively, indicating that the prepared EF-CDs and DEF-CDs are rich in hydrophilic -COOH, -NH2 and -OH groups, and these have high potential to form well-mixed packaging materials with PVA to produce biodegradable food packaging. In addition, the results of FTIR and XPS indicate that the -NH2 group is successfully incorporated into the CDs. Amino groups and amide bonds are crucial for enhancing the antibacterial performance of carbon dots (Travlou, et al., 2018), therefore the DEF-CDs possess great potential for application in antibacterial packaging.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of reaction temperature on the optical and structural performance of DEF-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The DEF-CDs that were prepared at 160, 200 and 240 °C (and respectively named DEF-CDs160, DEF-CDs200, and DEF-CDs240), were used to explore the effect of temperature on the optical performance of DEF-CDs. As shown in the Fig. 2d inset, the DEF-CDs200 solution exhibits a bright yellow color, and the DEF-CDs160 and DEF-CDs240 solution exhibits chestnut color. More importantly, the DEF-CDs160 solution produced a large amount of precipitation, which may have resulted from incomplete reaction products. In addition, the results show that two absorption peaks exhibit the same change trend (Fig. 2a-c). The absorption peak at 279 nm is enhanced from 0.35 to 1.01 with the increase in temperature from 160 °C to 200 °C, and then weakened to 0.89 when the preparation temperature increased to 240 °C. The absorption peak at 345 nm increased from 0.16 to 1.44 when the reaction temperature increased from 160 to 200 °C, and decreased to 0.92 at 240 °C. Correspondingly, the transmittance spectral results in Fig. 2d show that the prepared DEF-CDs200 exhibit the highest UV absorption of the three tested temperatures. To further elucidate the impact of temperature on UV absorption properties of DEF-CDs, FTIR analysis was first used to characterize the functional groups in the prepared DEF-CDs at three temperatures. The results in Fig. 2j and S5b show that with the temperature increase, the amide-I band stretching vibration peak at 1648 cm−1 (which was attributed to CO) first appeared in DEF-CDs200 (Guo, et al., 2024), and then decreased in DEF-CDs240. Theoretically, the absorption peaks of CDs centered at 279 nm and 345 nm are related to the π-π* transition of aromatic CC (279 nm) and the n-π* transition of CO (345 nm) (Qu, et al., 2014), respectively. Therefore, the FTIR results support the UV absorption properties of DEF-CDs160, DEF-CDs200 and DEF-CDs240. The DEF-CDs prepared at the three temperatures were further characterized by HRTEM. The results in Fig. 2e-g show that DEF-CDs160 and DEF-CDs200 exhibit better dispersion with sizes of 3.09 ± 0.66 nm and 1.85 ± 0.5 nm, respectively. DEF-CDs240 tend to aggregate (d = 2.84 ± 1.61 nm). Furthermore, DEF-CDs200 and DEF-CDs240 exhibit a discernible lattice fringe of 0.21 nm corresponding to the (100) plane of graphite, whereas the lattice fringe in DEF-CDs160 is less noticeable, indicating incomplete crystallization. These results are also supported by XRD analysis in Fig. 2h. Compared with DEF-CDs160, the diffraction peaks of DEF-CDs200 and DEF-CDs240 are relatively sharper, demonstrating their higher crystallinity and carbonization. In addition, XPS analysis was conducted to evaluate the respective carbonizations of DEF-CDs160, DEF-CDs200 and DEF-CDs240. As shown in Fig. 2i and S5a, and in Table S1, by increasing the temperature from 160 °C to 240 °C, the C content increased from 71.02 % to 75.79 %, and the O content decreased from 24.32 % to 19.24 %, resulting in the C/O ratio increased from 2.92 to 3.94. Therefore, the increase in C content and decrease in O content further confirms that DEF-CDs carbonization is dependent on the preparation temperature. In short, combining the aforementioned results, the DEF-CDs formation process at different temperatures can be described as in Fig. 2k. Ideal DEF-CDs consist of two parts: carbon graphite nuclei and amorphous regions. A precursor that undergoes inter-molecular condensation and polymerization at a low temperature, results in the formation of a polymer-like structure whose incomplete carbonization makes it difficult to form valuable carbon graphite nuclei, leading to poor optical performance. At an increased preparing temperature, the polymer-like structure is gradually transformed into ordered carbon graphite nuclei. When the content of ordered carbon graphite nuclei and the amorphous regions is in appropriate balance, DEF-CDs exhibit well dispersibility and optical performance. Excessive temperature, however, leads to the complete carbonization of the polymer-like structure, thereby compromise DEF-CDs stability, and decrease optical performance (Ren, Malfatti, & Innocenzi, 2020; Zhu, et al., 2013). In summary, we found that temperature significantly affects the formation process of DEF-CDs, thereby affecting their optical properties. Therefore, we chose to synthesize DEF-CDs at a temperature of 200 °C, which is beneficial for enhancing the UV absorption and stability of DEF-CDs, further ensuring their better performance in the UV shielding field.

The concentration of DEF and EDA for DEF-CDs synthesis was further optimized. First, serial DEF concentrations of 0, 20, 40, 80, and 160 mM were used to synthesize DEF-CDs. As shown in Fig. 3a, there is no significant absorption in the UV-A or UV-B region with a DEF concentration of 0 mM. When the DEF concentration increased from 20 mM to 40 mM, a gradually increasing absorption peak of 345 nm emerged. Interestingly, when the DEF concentration further increased from 40 mM to 80 mM, the DEF-CDs absorption peak is blue-shifted into the UV-B region. The UV blocking capacity of different DEF-CDs concentrations was calculated in Fig. 3c according to the transmittance spectrum in Fig. 3b. It is found that the DEF-CDs with a DEF concentration of 40 mM exhibits the optimal UV blocking capacity of 85.19 %. Furthermore, serial EDA concentrations of 0, 4, 40, 200, and 400 mM were used to further optimize the DEF-CDs. The results in Fig. 3d show that, with an increase in EDA concentration from 0 mM to 40 mM, the absorption peak in the UV-A region intensified. In addition, when the EDA concentration reached 200 mM, there is a sharp decrease in the absorption peak within the UV-A region, while an increase is observed within the HEBL region. This indicates that the DEF-CDs exhibit enhanced UV-A and HEBL absorption with an increase in nitrogen content. However, when EDA concentration further increased to 400 mM, the absorption peaks in the UV-A and HEBL regions both decreased compared with the EDA concentration of 200 mM, which may be because the strong alkalinity of EDA hindered the formation of DEF-CDs (J. Li, Ma, Xiao, & Zhao, 2019). Therefore, the transmittance spectrum in Fig. 3e-f shows that the DEF-CDs with an EDA concentration of 200 mM exhibits the optimal UV and HEBL blocking capacity (100 % shielding for the UV region, and 99.56 % for the HEBL region). These results indicate the promising application of DEF-CDs for nanoreinforcement of food package function on UV and HEBL prevention, yet using carbon dots on HEBL and ultraviolet prevention is challenging (W. Li, et al., 2025).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characterization of DEF-CDs/PVA films
ce:section.ce:para: Photooxidation of food caused by ultraviolet light results in off-flavor and nutrients loss (Ezati, et al., 2023). Moreover, a previous survey demonstrate that consumers are inclined to buy food with visible packaging (Guzman-Puyol, Benítez, & Heredia-Guerrero, 2022), therefore developing functional food package with UV shielding ability meanwhile maintaining transparency is important (Ezati, Rhim, Molaei, & Rezaei, 2022). In this study, DEF-CDs with contents of 0.2 %, 0.4 % and 0.8 % were respectively dispersed in PVA solution, and DEF-CDs/PVA films were prepared in accordance with the solvent casting method ( Scheme 1). The results in Fig. S6 shows that the PVA solution is transparent, and the DEF-CDs/PVA solution is yellow and transparent in appearance. However, unlike the DEF-CDs/PVA, a large amount of precipitation was found in the DEF/PVA due to the poor solubility of DEF. These result further demonstrate the good dispersibility and compatibility of DEF-CDs in the PVA matrix. In addition, Fig. 4a shows that the color of DEF-CDs/PVA films display high uniformity, and became brownish yellow with increasing DEF-CDs content. Conversely, the surface of the DEF/PVA film is rough in texture, introducing opacity (Fig. S7). Furthermore, the UV–vis absorption and transmittance spectrum in Fig. 4c-d shows that the PVA film exhibits negligible UV absorption ability. However, after the introduction of DEF-CDs, an obvious absorption in the UV and HEBL regions emerged in the DEF-CDs/PVA films, which increased as the DEF-CDs content increased. As a result, the transmittance spectra of the DEF-CDs/PVA films exhibit highly effective UV-to-HEBL blocking capacity, which relied on the DEF-CDs contents. The mechanism diagram of UV blocking of DEF-CDs/PVA films is shown in Fig. 4b, the UV absorption of the carbon dots is derived from the π-π* and the n-π* transition, which correspond to the transitions of fluorophores on DEF-CDs core and DEF-CDs surface, respectively. Carbon dots absorb ultraviolet light, causing electrons in CDs from the ground state to the excited state and emit fluorescence (Hu, et al., 2019). Finally, the 0.4 % DEF-CDs/PVA film is discovered to effectively shield the UV radiation with a blocking efficiency of 100 %, while also exhibiting 99.90 % blocking efficiency in the HEBL region. In addition, the 0.4 % DEF-CDs/PVA film exhibits a transmittance rate of 73.12 % at 600 nm wavelength, demonstrating the high transparency in the visible light region. Food packaging materials with good UV and high-energy blue light blocking properties can effectively prevent food spoilage caused by high-energy light radiation, which is of great significance to extend the shelf life of food (Yu, et al., 2022), but the bottleneck is that it is difficult to maintain the balance between high light blocking and transparency. The light blocking properties and transparency of DEF-CDs/PVA film were compared with other food packaging films in Table S2. It can be seen that the proposed DEF-CDs/PVA films not only exhibit better blocking ability of UV-A and UV-B, but also has obvious advantages in blocking HEBL, while maintaining good visible light transparency. The 0.4 % DEF-CDs/PVA in film has nearly 100 % UV and HEBL shielding capability along with fairly high transparency, therefore was used in further experiments. Moreover, the surface and cross-sectional morphology of the PVA film, DEF/PVA film, and DEF-CDs/PVA films were characterized by SEM. As shown in Fig. S8, the DEF-CDs/PVA films exhibit smoother surface structure than PVA film, and there are no significant changes in surface morphology when the DEF-CDs content increased from 0.2 % to 0.8 %. In addition, cross-sectional analysis of the DEF-CDs/PVA films also reveal continuous dense morphology. These results demonstrate strong compatibility between DEF-CDs and the PVA matrix, indicating that hydrogen bonding between -NH2, -COOH, -OH groups in DEF-CDs and -OH groups may enhance the dense morphology of DEF-CDs/PVA films. However, both surface and cross-sectional images of the DEF/PVA film exhibit a large number of insoluble particles, demonstrating the poor practicality of the DEF/PVA film.

FTIR characterization was used to analyze the interaction between the DEF-CDs and the PVA matrix. The FTIR spectra of PVA film and DEF-CDs/PVA films are presented in Fig. S9. It is evident that, compared with the FTIR spectrum of the PVA film, the FTIR spectra of the DEF-CDs/PVA films exhibit no new absorption peaks, indicating the absence of newly formed chemical bonds between carbon dots and PVA. In addition, with increased DEF-CDs content, slight blue shifts of the absorption peaks corresponding to O-H stretching vibrations are observed from 3260 cm−1 (PVA) to 3258 cm−1 (0.2 % DEF-CDs/PVA), 3259 cm−1 (0.4 % DEF-CDs/PVA) and 3250 cm−1 (0.8 % DEF-CDs/PVA). Furthermore, a similar blue shift of the absorption peaks related to O-H bending vibrations are observed from 1414 cm−1 (PVA) to 1413 cm−1 (0.2 % DEF-CDs/PVA), 1412 cm−1 (0.4 % DEF-CDs/PVA) and 1410 cm−1 (0.8 % DEF-CDs/PVA). These blue shifts in hydroxyl stretching and bending vibrations further confirm the hydrogen bond formation between DEF-CDs and the PVA matrix (Zhao, Zhang, Mujumdar, Adhikari, & Wang, 2022). Inadequate thermal stability of PVA film is a significant limitation that hinders its broader utilization in the packaging industry. However, the robust hydrogen-bond interaction between DEF-CDs and the PVA matrix suggests that DEF-CDs possess the potential to enhance the thermal stability of PVA film. Therefore, the effects of incorporating DEF-CDs on the thermal stability of PVA film were investigated by thermogravimetric curves (Fig. 4e) and derivative thermogravimetric curves (Fig. 4f). The weight loss rate and weight retention rate of PVA film and DEF-CDs/PVA films for each stage from 30 °C to 500 °C were calculated and presented in Table S3. It was observed that both the PVA film and DEF-CDs/PVA films underwent three similar degradation stages. First, the initial decomposition stage occurs at 30–130 °C due to water and glycerol evaporation. The second weight loss stage at 130–380 °C shows that composite films experienced significant weight loss during this period, which is primarily attributed to PVA degradation. After reaching 298 °C, the DEF-CDs/PVA films exhibit significant lower weight loss rates compared to pure PVA film, and an upward shift in the respective curves with the increase of DEF-CDs content, indicating that higher DEF-CDs content in PVA film results in improving thermal stability properties. In addition, the third weight loss stage occurred at 380–500 °C shows that the DEF-CDs/PVA films display higher weight loss rates compared to the PVA film, this is attributed to the degradation of DEF-CDs structures (Zhao, et al., 2022). Overall, these results indicate that DEF-CDs contribute towards enhancing the thermal stability properties of PVA films. Food packaging needs to meet sufficient mechanical tensile strength and elongation at break to prevent food damage and leakage during storage and transportation (Ezati & Rhim, 2021). The mechanical performance of DEF-CDs/PVA films were also evaluated (Fig. S10). Addition of DEF-CDs significantly improves TS and EB of PVA film, and the film with DEF-CDs/PVA at 0.4 % is optimal, which may be due to the strong hydrogen bonding between DEF-CDs and PVA (Rani, Kumar, Mandal, & Kumar, 2020). Addition of more DEF-CDs (0.8 %) in PVA film decreases the TS and EB performance, which may be due to the aggregation of CDs as reported previously (S. Chen et al. 2023; Koshy, et al., 2022; Q. Sun et al. 2024). The water contact angle was determined to evaluate the effect of DEF-CDs on the surface wettability of PVA films (Fig. S11). A tendency of more DEF-CDs reduces the water contact angle of composite films, and the reduction is not statistically significant when the DEF-CDs is below 0.4 %. Low oxygen permeability is a critical property of food packaging materials (Yi, Yuan, Xiao, Ren, & Zhai, 2023). The addition of DEF-CDs significantly enhances the oxygen barrier of PVA films (Fig. S12), and this is due to the formation of a dense hydrogen bond network structure between DEF-CDs and PVA, which increase the tortuosity of the oxygen diffusion pathway (Liu, et al., 2024). The oxygen permeability of 0.4 % DEF-CDs/PVA film is the lowest (0.227 cm3·μm/m2·d·kPa). However, when the addition of DEF-CDs increased to 0.8 %, the oxygen permeability is sharply increased, this may be due to the excess carboxyl and hydroxyl groups increase the interaction between oxygen and the film (Khan, Riahi, Kim, & Rhim, 2024).

ce:section.ce:section-title: UV-to-HEBL blocking evaluation of DEF-CDs/PVA films
ce:section.ce:para: CdSe/ZnS quantum dots with green fluorescence were used to visually demonstrate the UV blocking capacity of DEF-CDs/PVA films. Concretely, as shown in Fig. S13, the excitation spectra of CdSe/ZnS quantum dots are from 300 nm to 500 nm, which are in the UV-B, UV-A and HEBL regions, meaning that the exciting light in UV-B, UV-A and HEBL regions results in green fluorescent emissions from CdSe/ZnS quantum dots. The visual UV blocking analysis was described in Fig. 5a, a light source emitting UV-A was selected as the excitation source, the PVA film and DEF-CDs/PVA films were placed between the excitation light and the solution containing CdSe/ZnS quantum dots, and the intensity of the green fluorescence from the CdSe/ZnS quantum dots indicate the UV blocking efficacy of the tested films. Fig. 5b demonstrates that using PVA film as a barrier does not yield any significant disparity in green fluorescence compared to the blank control. However, in films with increasing DEF-CDs content, the green fluorescence diminished until it is undetectable when the DEF-CDs content reaches 0.4 %. This finding strongly confirms that the DEF-CDs/PVA films have good UV and HEBL blocking efficacy.

Riboflavin is a prototypical photosensitizer, which can be rapidly degraded to colorless upon exposure to the ultraviolet light. Therefore, the riboflavin was employed in this study to further investigate the UV blocking capacity of DEF-CDs/PVA films. The transparent bottle loaded with riboflavin was coated with the proposed films and subsequently subjected to UV irradiation at a power of 18 W for a duration of 32 hours (Fig. 5c). The result in Fig. 5e shows that riboflavin, when covered by a PVA film, exhibits a similar trend to the blank control, which turned colorless after 24 hours. However, when covered with DEF-CDs/PVA films, the riboflavin maintains its initial yellow color even after 32 hours. Furthermore, the change of the absorption peak (445 nm) for riboflavin was recorded in Fig. 5d, the OD445 nm for the PVA film and blank groups decreased from 0.59 to 0.06 (for PVA film) and from 0.60 to 0.06 (for blank group) as the UV irradiation time increased to 32 h. However, the OD445 nm for the DEF-CDs/PVA film groups show no significant changes after 32 h. These results demonstrate the significant potential of DEF-CDs/PVA films in preventing photosensitizer degradation, owing to their exceptional UV blocking capacity.

To evaluate the anti-aging performance of the proposed DEF-CDs/PVA films, the 0.4 % DEF-CDs/PVA film was exposed to UV irradiation (18 W) for 40 days. As shown in Fig. S14 and Fig. 5f, the UV–vis absorption and transmittance spectrum of the 0.4 % DEF-CDs/PVA films shows no significant changes during the 40 days. Furthermore, the transmittance for three representative wavelengths of 450 nm, 500 nm, and 600 nm were respectively counted in Fig. 5g. The average transmittance during the 40 days was counted as 69.8 ± 1.7 % at 600 nm, 33.2 ± 1.9 % at 500 nm, 0 ± 0 % at 450 nm. The negligible standard deviation of the average transmittance for these three representative wavelengths further demonstrate the exceptional photostability of the DEF-CDs/PVA films.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of antibacterial capacity of DEF-CDs/PVA films
ce:section.ce:para: The antibacterial capacity of the DEF-CDs/PVA films were further evaluated by testing E. coli O157:H7 (gram-negative) and S. aureus (gram-positive). First, the antibacterial capacity of the proposed DEF-CDs were evaluated by incubating with E. coli O157:H7 and S. aureus at different concentrations (0, 1, 2 and 4 mg/mL, respectively). The colony count was determined using the plate counting method. As shown in Fig. 6a-b, the DEF-CDs exhibit remarkable antibacterial activity against E. coli O157:H7 and S. aureus. Interestingly, a 100 % inhibition rate for the E. coli O157:H7 emerged with the DEF-CDs concentration of 4 mg/mL, and for the S. aureus, lower DEF-CDs concentration of 2 mg/mL exhibits a 100 % inhibition rate, meaning that DEF-CDs may have better antibacterial activity versus gram-positive bacteria. The stronger negative surface charges of gram-positive bacteria may account for this phenomenon, facilitating enhanced binding of DEF-CDs (Cremin, et al., 2020). Furthermore, the antibacterial activity of DEF-CDs/PVA was evaluated using the Oxford cup method, and the diameter of the inhibition zone was used to characterize the antibacterial activity of DEF-CDs/PVA. The results in Fig. 6c-d show that PVA exhibits no inhibition zone for the E. coli O157:H7 and S. aureus. However, the inhibition zone is increased (8 ± 0 mm to 11.4 ± 0.5 mm for E. coli O157:H7, and 14.6 ± 0.8 mm to 22.2 ± 0.8 mm for S. aureus) as the DEF-CDs/PVA concentration increased from 4 mg/mL to 16 mg/mL, indicating the significant potential of DEF-CDs/PVA films in preventing the bacterial pollution, owing to their remarkable antibacterial capacity. The mechanism diagram of antibacterial of DEF-CDs/PVA films is shown in Fig. 6e, the protic forms of amide bonds and amino functional groups on the surface of CDs have electrostatic interaction with the bacterial cell membranes, resulting in membrane damage and bacterial death (Travlou, et al., 2018).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of DEF-CDs/PVA films to food preservation
ce:section.ce:para: The results in Fig. S15 indicate that, compared to the control group, there is no significant difference in the viability of C3H10T1/2 cells after co-cultured with DEF-CDs, even at concentration as high as 250 μg/mL, meaning the high biocompatibility and safety of this proposed DEF-CDs. To evaluate the practical application of DEF-CDs/PVA films, we investigated their protective effects on jujubes, which are prone to photooxidative damage thus leading to browning and decay. Concretely, jujubes covered with the proposed films were exposed to the UV irradiation (18 W) for 7 d, and the appearance of the jujubes were recorded by digital camera after 1 d, 3 d, 5 d, and 7 d. The results in Fig. 7a demonstrate a significant alleviation of the brown stain trends in jujubes after being protected by the DEF-CDs/PVA films. Especially on the 7th day, the jujubes protected by the 0.8 % DEF-CDs/PVA film retains their yellow-green color, while those in the blank control and PVA film groups turned completely brownish-black. Jujubes produces reactive oxygen species (ROS) under ultraviolet radiation, which causes degradation of chlorophyll, leading to the color change (Zhao, et al., 2022), whereas the process is significantly slowed down due to the fact that DEF-CDs/PVA films can greatly block UV radiation. Furthermore, the loss of hardness is documented in Fig. 7b, because hardness served as a critical indicator for evaluating fruit freshness and maturity. The results demonstrate that the rate of hardness loss of jujubes decreased as the DEF-CDs content increased, indicating a longer storage life.

The susceptibility of strawberries to microbial contamination during storage makes them an ideal representative fruit for evaluating the antimicrobial performance of the DEF-CDs/PVA films. As shown in Fig. 7c, with increased storage time, the strawberries in the blank control and PVA film groups exhibits significant shrinkage and fungal infection. However, with the protection of DEF-CDs/PVA films, the appearance and color of the strawberries on 7th day shows no apparent difference compared to those on the first day, and no fungal infection is observed in DEF-CDs/PVA protected strawberries. The results indicate the enhanced antimicrobial capacity of the proposed DEF-CDs/PVA films, which effectively inhibit strawberry decay and significantly extend their shelf life. Furthermore, the results in Fig. 7d demonstrate that the weight loss of strawberries protected by the DEF-CDs/PVA films is lower than that in both the blank control and PVA film groups. The weight loss of the strawberries is primarily due to the microbial consumption and water evaporation (Amoroso, et al., 2022; Peretto, et al., 2014). The antibacterial capacity and dense structure resulting from the hydrogen bonds of DEF-CDs/PVA films effectively prevent microbial consumption and water evaporation, thus leading to the lower the weight loss rate.

Milk photooxidation is a big concern in the dairy industry, and the excitation of photosensitizers (tetrapyrroles) in milk by ultraviolet radiation is the main cause. Photooxidized milk leads to a deterioration in quality and shorter shelf life. In addition, a recent publication reported significant oxidative stress in mice livers after gavage administration of photooxidized milk, indicating a possible health threat to humans (S. Tan, et al., 2024). Previous publication reported four biomarkers to evaluate the extent of milk photooxidation, i.e. riboflavin, lumichrome, uric acid, and indole-3-carboxaldehyde (IAId) (D. Tan, et al., 2021). In this study, the efficacy of the proposed DEF-CDs/PVA films in protecting milk against photooxidation reactions was evaluated by use of these four biomarkers (Fig. 8a). As shown in Fig. 8b, the riboflavin and uric acid contents in the blank control and PVA film groups decreased, as the illumination time increased from 0 h to 72 h. However, with increased DEF-CDs content, the decreasing trend of riboflavin and uric acid content is gradually weakened, and even exhibits no significant difference compared that of day 0 for the 0.8 % DEF-CDs/PVA film. Inversely, the lumichrome and IAId contents in the blank control and PVA film groups increased sharply as the illumination time increased from 0 h to 72 h. However, with the protection of the DEF-CDs/PVA films, the lumichrome and IAId content shows no significant difference at 72 h compared to those at 0 h. These findings demonstrate that the DEF-CDs/PVA films effectively inhibit the photooxidation of milk due to their excellent UV barrier properties, therefore offering remarkable potential in application in dairy industry.

In summary, we have successfully synthesized a brand new type of carbon dots by a facile hydrothermal method using ferulate ester derivatives, and further expounded the influencing mechanism of the ferulate ester derivative structure, temperature and precursor content on the optical performance of the DEF-CDs. The proposed DEF-CDs demonstrate effective UV-to-HEBL blocking and antibacterial capabilities, which can be attributed to the directional design of the structure. After incorporating the DEF-CDs into the PVA matrix to fabricate uniformly dispersed composite films, the proposed DEF-CDs/PVA films exhibit outstanding UV-to-HEBL blocking efficiency (100 % blocking efficiency for UV, and 99.90 % blocking efficiency for HEBL), high transparency, anti-aging properties, thermal stability, and antibacterial activity. The DEF-CDs/PVA films exhibit outstanding properties related to extending the shelf life of jujubes and strawberries, as well as preventing the photooxidation of milk. This work elucidates the application of a nanomaterial in the fabrication of food packaging with multifunctional performance.

This study was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (32230083), the Postdoctoral Fellowship Program of CPSF (GZC20230176), the fund of Cultivation Project of Double First-Class Disciplines of Food Science and Engineering, Beijing Technology & Business University (BTBUKF202207); 2024 Graduate Research Ability Enhancement Program, Beijing Technology & Business University (19008024042).
